did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a semi-supervised deep learning method based on convolutional autoencoders to segment nests of melanocytes in histopathological images of skin specimens, which is a task in dermatopathology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a semi-supervised segmentation method using convolutional autoencoders for nests of melanocytes. It details a specific methodology, dataset preparation, training procedures, and reports new experimental results (Dice similarity coefficient 0.81, sensitivity 0.76, specificity 0.94) on a histopathological dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Semi-Supervised Nests of Melanocytes Segmentation Method Using Convolutional Autoencoders
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of segmenting nests of melanocytes in histopathological images of H&E-stained skin specimens, a critical task for distinguishing benign nevi from malignant melanoma. The authors propose a semi-supervised deep learning method utilizing a convolutional autoencoder architecture with two training stages: unsupervised encoding and supervised decoding with limited ground-truth data. To overcome the scarcity of labeled datasets, the approach incorporates data augmentation and specific autoencoder adaptations. The model was evaluated on a custom dataset of skin histopathology images to assess its segmentation performance. Experimental results demonstrated that the method achieved a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.81, sensitivity of 0.76, and specificity of 0.94. These metrics represent a state-of-the-art result for this specific segmentation task, validating the effectiveness of the proposed semi-supervised approach.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on distinguishing benign nevi from malignant melanoma. The segmentation method targets nests of melanocytes, which is a key diagnostic criterion for differentiating melanoma from nevi. Melanoma is described as the most aggressive and lethal skin cancer.

paper_terms: junctional dysplastic nevus with severe atypia
lesion_types_studied.details: The study aims to segment nests of nevus cells to aid in the differential diagnosis between benign nevi and malignant melanoma. The paper discusses the histopathological features of nevus cells and nests.

all_task_types.target: Nests of melanocytes (nevus cells) in histopathological images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a semi-supervised deep learning method using convolutional autoencoders to segment nests of melanocytes. The task involves producing pixel-wise masks for the nests regions. Performance is evaluated using the Dice similarity coefficient (0.81), sensitivity (0.76), and specificity (0.94), which are standard metrics for segmentation tasks comparing predicted masks against ground-truth annotations.

datasets_used.name: Custom Histopathological Dataset from University Hospital in Krakow and Jagiellonian University Medical College
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of Whole Slide Images (WSIs) of H&E-stained skin specimens obtained through scientific cooperation with the University Hospital in Krakow and the Chair of Pathomorphology of Jagiellonian University Medical College. The images were captured at 10x magnification (0.44 µm/px) using an Axio Scan.Z1 slide scanner. The dataset includes 70 WSIs of selected melanocytic lesions. Ground truth segmentation masks for nests of nevus cells were manually prepared by an experienced dermatopathologist for a subset of 39 WSIs.

use.training: Used for semi-supervised training of the convolutional autoencoder. The method utilized data augmentation and a two-stage training process (unsupervised encoding followed by supervised decoding with limited ground-truth data). Specific split counts for training/validation/testing are not reported; the paper notes the scarcity of labeled data as a key challenge.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the semi-supervised approach implies validation during the supervised decoding stage using the limited ground-truth data.
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the segmentation performance. The 39 WSIs with manually prepared ground truth masks were used to verify results and calculate metrics (Dice similarity coefficient 0.81, sensitivity 0.76, specificity 0.94).
use.other: The remaining 31 WSIs (70 total - 39 with ground truth) were likely used in the unsupervised encoding phase or as unlabeled data for the semi-supervised approach, though the exact count used for unsupervised pre-training is not explicitly separated from the total.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the degree of overlapping between the experimental segmentation masks and the manual ground-truth segmentations.
To calculate the proportion of correctly detected nests' structure pixels (true positives) among all actual positive pixels.
To calculate the proportion of correctly identified background pixels (true negatives) among all actual negative pixels.
To divide the dataset into training, validation, and test sets while maintaining the proportion of labeled to unlabeled images constant across sets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (Dice similarity coefficient, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical inference.

Custom Histopathological Dataset (Univer.experiment: Semi-supervised segmentation of nests of melanocytes using Convolutional Autoencoder
Custom Histopathological Dataset (Univer.notes: The method achieved state-of-the-art results for this specific task. The paper notes that the small ground-truth database was segmented manually. Misclassified areas were observed on the border between patches and in small areas of the nests containing only a few cells. No other works or solutions for this specific segmentation task were reported for comparison.

architecture_used.pretraining: Unsupervised encoding stage using mean squared error loss on 49 images (223,274 patches) to obtain encoder weights; weights frozen for supervised decoding stage.

classification_or_output_head.purpose: Binary classification (probability map for nest pixels)

preprocessing_techniques: Image patch extraction: Whole Slide Images (WSIs) were split into small image patches of size 128 × 128 pixels.
Non-overlapping extraction for negative samples: For parts of images where nests were not marked, consecutive examples were extracted with a 128 px step in width and height without overlapping.
Overlapping extraction for positive samples: When a nest part was found during patch extraction, overlapping was applied to produce more examples of the positive class.
Data augmentation: Rotation of particular parts by 45 degrees to generate four more examples for balancing classes.

Stratified dataset splitting: The dataset was divided into training, validation, and test parts at the image level (not patch level) before patch extraction to maintain the proportion of labeled to unlabeled images.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation of particular parts of the image by 45 degrees to generate four more examples, used to balance classes in the dataset.
Applied during patch extraction when a nest part was found to produce more examples of the positive class (nests), serving as a form of sampling-based augmentation for class balancing.

conclusions: The proposed semi-supervised deep learning method using convolutional autoencoders is effective for segmenting nests of melanocytes in histopathological images, particularly in scenarios with limited ground-truth data.
The method achieved a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.81, sensitivity of 0.76, and specificity of 0.94, representing a state-of-the-art result for this specific segmentation task.

The two-stage training approach (unsupervised encoding followed by supervised decoding with limited ground-truth data) allows for effective adaptation of autoencoders to small datasets through data augmentation and architectural specification.
To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first described method to segment the nests region of melanocytes in histopathological images of H&E-stained skin specimens.
The method addresses the challenge of subjectivity and labor intensity in traditional manual histopathological examination by providing an automatic, accurate, and reproducible segmentation tool.
Misclassifications were observed primarily on the borders between patches and in small areas of nests containing only a few cells, indicating areas for potential improvement.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset (70 WSIs, 39 with ground truth) from the University Hospital in Krakow, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population or scanner bias.
No external validation was performed; the authors explicitly state plans to validate on a larger dataset from various laboratories in future work.
The method sacrifices global spatial context by processing non-overlapping 128x128 pixel patches, which may miss broader architectural features relevant to diagnosis.
Performance limitations were noted at patch borders and in small nests containing few cells, indicating potential failure modes for subtle or fragmented lesions.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the reported metrics (Dice 0.81, Sensitivity 0.76, Specificity 0.94).
The tool is positioned as an assistive aid for pathologists rather than a standalone diagnostic system, particularly for difficult cases like junctional dysplastic nevus vs. melanoma in situ.
Computational intensity is acknowledged as a barrier to real-time deployment, with the authors noting the need for hardware accelerators for clinical integration.
